narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, an LSAT score of 29.50, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the 1st decile—meaning your early grades placed you at the lower end compared to your peers, where averages hover around the 5th or 6th decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 56.1% chance that you would fail the bar exam, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven assessment to help the school manage outcomes for students like you. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom tier, as lower performance there strongly signals challenges in sustaining the academic rigor needed for bar success, unlike the typical student's mid-range standing. Next in impact was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward passing—your 3.40 exceeds the average of 3.21, showing a solid foundation that the model views positively. Rounding out the top influences, your Year 1 decile in the 1st position nudged things toward failure, reinforcing early struggles that often correlate with bar exam hurdles in the dataset. While your LSAT score of 29.50 is below the average 36.70 and your middle-income background aligns with norms, they played lesser roles here compared to those decile rankings. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students mainly due to those low early deciles, even as your GPA provides a counterbalance. This isn't set in stone—many students overcome similar starts through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, seeking academic advising to improve your decile performance if possible, or exploring programs with strong bar prep support tailored for full-time Hispanic students like yourself. Building a narrative of upward momentum in a reapplication could shift the model's view significantly. You're clearly motivated, and with strategic steps, law school doors can reopen.
